label: delphi-r1-p7
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The Sierra Vista Municipal Water District (invented) serves ~140,000 people in a chronically drought-stressed service area. After a rate study and a majority-protest hearing that drew protests from ~9% of parcels, the board adopted a "Water Resilience Surcharge": $0.85 per hundred cubic feet on residential usage above Tier 2 (the surcharge falls on roughly the top 22% of residential users), projected to raise $4.1M/year into a restricted fund spent as follows: 55% on turf-replacement and high-efficiency-appliance rebates that prioritize low-income customers; 30% on debt service for a recycled-water plant; 15% on "water ambassador" conservation-education staff.

The rate study concludes that high-tier consumption imposes marginal supply costs roughly 1.6× base-tier costs (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases), but it derives the $0.85 figure in part from what it calls "conservation-incentive pricing" rather than strictly from cost allocation, and the district concedes the rebate program mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge. A ratepayer association has sued, alleging the surcharge exceeds the proportional cost of service attributable to the payers' parcels in violation of article XIII D, section 6(b) of the California Constitution, citing Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano. The district defends the structure under its conservation mandate (Cal. Const. art. X, § 2; Water Code § 375) and argues that, in the alternative, the surcharge is not a property-related fee at all.

## The question

Does the Water Resilience Surcharge violate article XIII D, section 6(b)'s cost-of-service and proportionality requirements? How does Capistrano apply to a tier surcharge that is partly cost-justified (1.6×) and partly incentive-priced, with proceeds partially redistributed as rebates to non-payers? Can article X, section 2's conservation mandate be reconciled with 218, or does 218 win? Is there a colorable argument the surcharge is a tax requiring voter approval (Prop 26), or a non-property-related charge? What is the litigation exposure (refunds, fees) and how could the structure be repaired?

## Proposition to assess

"A court would invalidate the Water Resilience Surcharge, in whole or in substantial part, as violating the cost-of-service requirements of California Constitution article XIII D, section 6(b)."

Estimate the probability (0–100) that this proposition is correct, with your reasoning. rationales = your independent load-bearing reasons, one sentence each. authorities = specific authorities you are actually relying on (they will be flagged for human verification). biggest_uncertainty = the single thing you are least sure of.

Analytical starting point for you: Begin from the advice a cautious public-agency counsel would give, then ask whether caution is legally required or merely prudent.
text: {"probability":70,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"More likely than not that a court finds the Water Resilience Surcharge violates Article XIII D, section 6(b) in whole or substantial part. The most plausible path to invalidation is not wholesale rejection of tiered pricing but a finding that (a) the increment of the $0.85 charge attributable to \"conservation-incentive pricing\" beyond what the 1.6x documented cost differential supports is unconstitutional under Capistrano, and/or (b) the revenue-use structure (55% funding rebates that mostly benefit non-payors) breaches the proportional-cost-of-service and aggregate-revenue limits of 6(b)(1) and 6(b)(3). The district's own rate-study language and factual concessions are unusually damaging admissions against a defendant that bears the burden of proof, but the genuine 1.6x marginal-cost study gives it a real partial defense, making a full, clean survival of the surcharge unlikely but a total wipeout also less likely than a partial judicial rebalancing — which still satisfies this proposition's \"in whole or substantial part\" standard.","rationales":["The rate study's own admission that $0.85 was derived 'in part' from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strictly cost allocation lands close to the practice Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 held unconstitutional — pricing tiers by demand-suppression goals rather than actual documented cost differentials.","Article XIII D, section 6(b) places the burden of proof on the agency in any legal action contesting a fee's validity, and the district's own concessions (mixed-motive pricing, rebate program benefiting non-payors) function as evidence undermining its ability to meet that burden rather than helping it.","The concession that 55% of revenue funds rebates that 'mostly benefit' customers other than the surcharge payors invites an independent proportionality attack under section 6(b)(1)/(3), since using one class's payments to subsidize another class's appliances/turf looks more like redistribution than recovery of 'the proportional cost of the service attributable to the parcel.'","Cutting the other way, the district has a genuine documented 1.6x marginal-cost differential (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases) — exactly the kind of legitimate cost linkage Capistrano itself recognized as a valid basis for tiered pricing — giving the district a partial, non-frivolous defense that could preserve a cost-justified core of the surcharge.","Because the proposition is satisfied by invalidation 'in whole or in substantial part,' even a partial remedy (severing the above-1.6x increment or the rebate-funded share of revenue) would render the proposition true, which meaningfully raises the probability compared to a stricter all-or-nothing framing."],"authorities":["Cal. Const. art. XIII D, § 6(b)(1)-(5)","Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493","Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205","Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Management Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586","City of Palmdale v. Palmdale Water Dist. (2011) 198 Cal.App.4th 926","Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water Dist. (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372","Cal. Const. art. X, § 2; Water Code § 375 (conservation mandate, likely does not override art. XIII D per Capistrano reasoning)","Cal. Const. art. XIII C (Prop 26) — alternative tax-classification theory if surcharge deemed not a valid cost-based property-related fee"],"biggest_uncertainty":"Whether a reviewing court will treat the \"conservation-incentive pricing\" language in the rate study as merely an additional policy rationale layered atop an otherwise-adequate 1.6x cost-of-service study (surviving under a reasonableness standard), or as proof that the $0.85 figure itself was not actually derived from cost allocation (fatal under Capistrano) — this characterization is likely outcome-determinative and turns on rate-study methodology details not specified in the hypothetical."}

data.position_summary: More likely than not that a court finds the Water Resilience Surcharge violates Article XIII D, section 6(b) in whole or substantial part. The most plausible path to invalidation is not wholesale rejection of tiered pricing but a finding that (a) the increment of the $0.85 charge attributable to "conservation-incentive pricing" beyond what the 1.6x documented cost differential supports is unconstitutional under Capistrano, and/or (b) the revenue-use structure (55% funding rebates that mostly benefit non-payors) breaches the proportional-cost-of-service and aggregate-revenue limits of 6(b)(1) and 6(b)(3). The district's own rate-study language and factual concessions are unusually damaging admissions against a defendant that bears the burden of proof, but the genuine 1.6x marginal-cost study gives it a real partial defense, making a full, clean survival of the surcharge unlikely but a total wipeout also less likely than a partial judicial rebalancing — which still satisfies this proposition's "in whole or substantial part" standard.
rationales: The rate study's own admission that $0.85 was derived 'in part' from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strictly cost allocation lands close to the practice Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 held unconstitutional — pricing tiers by demand-suppression goals rather than actual documented cost differentials.

Article XIII D, section 6(b) places the burden of proof on the agency in any legal action contesting a fee's validity, and the district's own concessions (mixed-motive pricing, rebate program benefiting non-payors) function as evidence undermining its ability to meet that burden rather than helping it.

The concession that 55% of revenue funds rebates that 'mostly benefit' customers other than the surcharge payors invites an independent proportionality attack under section 6(b)(1)/(3), since using one class's payments to subsidize another class's appliances/turf looks more like redistribution than recovery of 'the proportional cost of the service attributable to the parcel.'

Cutting the other way, the district has a genuine documented 1.6x marginal-cost differential (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases) — exactly the kind of legitimate cost linkage Capistrano itself recognized as a valid basis for tiered pricing — giving the district a partial, non-frivolous defense that could preserve a cost-justified core of the surcharge.

Because the proposition is satisfied by invalidation 'in whole or in substantial part,' even a partial remedy (severing the above-1.6x increment or the rebate-funded share of revenue) would render the proposition true, which meaningfully raises the probability compared to a stricter all-or-nothing framing.
authorities: Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493
Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205
Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Management Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586
City of Palmdale v. Palmdale Water Dist. (2011) 198 Cal.App.4th 926
Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water Dist. (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372
Cal. Const. art. X, § 2; Water Code § 375 (conservation mandate, likely does not override art. XIII D per Capistrano reasoning)
Cal. Const. art. XIII C (Prop 26) — alternative tax-classification theory if surcharge deemed not a valid cost-based property-related fee
data.biggest_uncertainty: Whether a reviewing court will treat the "conservation-incentive pricing" language in the rate study as merely an additional policy rationale layered atop an otherwise-adequate 1.6x cost-of-service study (surviving under a reasonableness standard), or as proof that the $0.85 figure itself was not actually derived from cost allocation (fatal under Capistrano) — this characterization is likely outcome-determinative and turns on rate-study methodology details not specified in the hypothetical.
